---
title: "R Setup and Maintenance"
date: "January 2026"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
number-sections: false
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    page-layout: full
editor: visual
---

## Fresh Setup

If you are getting your machine set up for using R for the first time, follow these steps below.

**1) Install R**

-   Download and install R from CRAN: [**https://cran.r-project.org/**](https://cran.r-project.org/)

-   You should be able to install in a user directory and not require admin privileges.

**2) Install R tools**

-   Install Rtools: [**https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/**](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/)

**3) Install Poist RStudio or Positron**

-   RStudio and Poistron are both Integrated Developent Environments for data science from Posit.

-   RStudio has been the standard for R data science. Positron is the next generation successor. RStudio will still be maintained and available for many year, however Posit's development efforts will be on Positron.

-   If you are starting fresh and learning (or re-learning R), Positron may be the best avenue. Download and install here: [Positron](https://positron.posit.co/).

-   Or download and install RStudio Desktop (Posit): [**https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/**](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/)

-   Launch RStudio/Positron and confirm it finds your R installation.

**3) Install Git**

-   Download Git for your OS: [**https://git-scm.com/**](https://git-scm.com/) and install.

**4) Create a GitHub account**

-   Go to [**https://github.com/**](https://github.com/) and sign up for an account.

-   Request to join the TTC-ENW GitHub organization: <https://github.com/TTC-ENW>

**5) Github Setup**

-   Use these instructions for setting up RStudio to use Git. <https://happygitwithr.com/>
-   For Positron, I don't see any single source that i the perfect guide. Search for instructions.

## Keeping R Up To Date

The info bar above the console window indicates the R version you are using. When a new version of R is available, it must be done in the basic R interface (RGui) (i.e., not in RStudio).

```{r}
#| eval: false

install.packages("installr")
library(installr)
updateR()
```

After R has been updated in the basic R interface, you will probably need to tell RStudio where to find the version you would like to use (usually the latest) under Tools \> Global Options.

## Keeping RStudio and Positron Up To Date

RStudio will let you know when its time. Positron automatically checks for updates.

## Package Updates

Its good to keep your packages up to date (unless there is a need to maintain an earlier version to maintain compatibility for a specific project and use). There will be a time when a package you are using is not working because some of the dependencies have been updated and your main package has not (or vice versa).

In RStudio, use the menu item "Check for Package Updates".

In Positron, there is no built-in package helper. Can use `old.packages()` to list them and then decide to re-install.

## Good Practices

-   New repositories should start with a 2-letter code. Use underscores to separate words. All lower case.

-   Add a README.md and .gitignore (using R template).

-   Repositories should be private, except for material that is not sensitive, like this learning and reference repo.

-   Use short, descriptive commit messages.

-   Push often and pull before working to avoid conflicts. If there will be more than one person working with a repo at the same time, best to make a new branch, complete your updates, and then do a pull-request to merge with main.
